system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

  { key: 'Referrer-Policy', value: 'strict-origin-when-cross-origin' },
  { key: 'Permissions-Policy', value: 'camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=()' },
]

const config: NextConfig = {
  output: "standalone",
  poweredByHeader: false,
  async headers() {
    return [
      { source: '/(.*)', headers: securityHeaders },
      { source: '/images/(.*)', headers: [{ key: 'Cache-Control', value: 'public, max-age=31536000, immutable' }] },
    ]
  },
  async redirects() {
    return [

}

Editing `sites/[tenant]/tokens.json` requires `npm run prebuild && npm run build` before Docker build. Fix: `docker service scale paragu-ai-builder_web=1 && sleep 15 && docker service scale paragu-ai-builder_web=3` to explicitly kill all old replicas. Verify with `docker ps --filter name=paragu-ai-builder --format \"{{.ID}} {{.Image}} {{.Status}}\"` — all must show the same SHA. **IMPORTANT:** after scaling down/up, wait until Docker Hub shows all containers with the same image SHA before declaring success. Run `docker service logs paragu-ai-builder_web --tail 5 | grep -iE \"Ready|error|ReferenceError\"` to confirm no stale instances are still receiving traffic. If an old instance with a ReferenceError keeps appearing in logs, the load balancer is still routing to it — the scale-down didn't fully evict it. Force with `docker service update --force --image paragu-ai:prod paragu-ai-builder_web`. **Deploy trap — `docker service update --force` does NOT rebuild the image.** It only restarts containers with the EXISTING image tag. Component changes ALWAYS need the full build cycle. **Stale container trap after update:** `docker service update --force` creates new containers but the OLD containers may STILL receive HTTP traffic from the load balancer if they haven't fully shut down. Test by checking `docker service logs paragu-ai-builder_web --tail 5 | grep "ReferenceError"` after deploy — if present, the fix is to patch both locations and redeploy. ## Subagent Page Imports Out of Sync (Expanded) When using `delegate_task` to create page files, the subagent may use different import paths or component names than actual files on disk. **This happened in BOTH dayah and elviajero builds** — the subagent created `.js` files instead of `.tsx`, imported `@/components/faq-section` instead of `@/components/faq`, and left `Header.js`/`Footer.js`/`WhatsAppFloat.js` alongside the `.tsx` versions. **Verification command after ANY subagent page creation:** ```bash # 1. Implementation order:** es.json (data layer) → lib/ (utilities) → components/ → pages/ → layout/ → globals.css. This avoids cascading changes. **4. The sequence: deploy looks successful (no errors), service shows 2/2 replicas, overlay test returns 200 — but external HTTPS returns 000. Debug checklist: 1. **Check Traefik labels are applied:** `docker service inspect {stack_name}_web --format '{{json .Spec.TaskTemplate.ContainerSpec.Labels}}' | python3 -m json.tool` — if only `com.docker.stack.namespace` is present, the labels were NOT applied. This happens when `docker stack deploy` receives a compose file with `build:` instead of `image:` — it silently drops `deploy.labels` too. 2. **Check SSL cert status:** `docker service logs traefik_traefik --tail 20 2>&1 | grep -i '{domain}'` — if you see `rateLimited`, Let's Encrypt has banned the domain for ~1h due to too many failed authorization attempts. Cloudflare proxy mode + LetsEncrypt HTTP-01 = the challenge hits Cloudflare's IP, not the origin, causing repeated failures. 3. **Stack name collision:** If a client was deployed with different stack names (e.g. `alejandro_villayamor` then `alejandro`), the OLD Traefik service discovery still has the old stack's container info. `docker stack rm {old_stack}` resolves it. Always remove ALL prior stacks before redeploying a migrated client. 4. **Certificate verification lag:** Even after Traefik issues a cert, it can take 30-60 seconds for Cloudflare's edge to cache the new cert. The initial requests hit 526 while propagation completes. **Fix for LetsEncrypt rate limit (alejandro-villamayor case):** Switch Cloudflare SSL/TLS to "Flexible" (Dashboard → SSL/TLS → Flexible) or set the DNS record to grey cloud (DNS only) until the cert is issued, then switch back. ## Nexa Architecture — Reference for Multi-Locale Standalone Sites ``` nexa.paragu-ai.com ├── content/ │ ├── en.json (79KB — source of truth for builds) │ ├── es.json (83KB) │ ├── nl.json (82KB) │ ├── de.json (82KB) │ └── resolved.json (4KB — pre-resolved EN data for components) ├── lib/ │ └── resolve.ts (build-time content resolver — NOT used at runtime) ├── app/ │ ├── page.tsx (6 sections: hero + programs + pillars + process + testimonials + CTA) │ ├── programs/page.tsx (4 tiers with pricing, inline data) │ ├── about/page.tsx (story + 6 differentiators) │ ├── faq/page.tsx (7 accordion Q&A, inline) │ ├── blog/page.tsx (8 post cards, inline) │ ├── contact/page.tsx (Calendly + WhatsApp buttons) │ ├── [...slug]/page.tsx (catch-all for 7+ stub pages) │ └── not-found.tsx ├── components/ (header.tsx, footer.tsx, whatsapp-float.tsx) └── public/images/ (187 WebP images, 0 PNGs) ``` **Critical:** The resolver is a BUILD-TIME tool only. Running it at import time in components means every request loads and resolves 80KB of JSON. The resolved output is written to `content/resolved.json` and components import THAT file instead. The `lib/resolve.ts` file exists for regeneration, not runtime use. **Cleanup reference:** `references/standalone-asset-audit.md` for the full asset audit process (identifying PNG duplicates, orphan SVGs, unused brand files). ### Common Standalone App Pitfalls 1. **Lucide v1.14+ dropped brand icons** — `Instagram`, `Facebook`, `Twitter` are removed. Import error: `Export Facebook doesn't exist in target module`. Fix: define inline SVG components in footer.tsx. See `references/lucide-brand-icons-workaround.md`. 2. **Next.js 16 uses `src/` directory by default** — `create-next-app` creates `src/app/` not `app/`. Content at `src/content/es.json` needs Dockerfile to copy `src/content/` and layout imports use `@/content/es.json`. 3. **Archived repos must be unarchived before push** — `gh api repos/org/repo -X PATCH -F archived=false`. Check with `gh repo view org/repo --json isArchived`. 4. **`delegate_task` subagent fails with 401**** — OpenRouter/provider auth errors prevent parallel execution. When subagent calls fail, fall back to sequential execution directly in the main session. The subagent system uses a different model (claude-sonnet-4 by default) which may be misconfigured or hitting rate limits. If `delegate_task` returns `Error code: 401 - {'error': {'message': 'User not found.'}}`, debug with `hermes config get models` or work around with terminal + `python3 << 'PYEOF' ... PYEOF` for batch operations. 2. **Auto-generated lead sites are TWO versions behind** — The PAB generator produces sites with an older template missing: `HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0` in Dockerfile, `traefik.docker.network=agent-net` label, healthchecks, non-root user, security headers, 404/error pages, robots/sitemap. Always apply `references/lead-site-deployment-checklist.md` from the standalone-ecommerce-store skill when deploying lead-generated sites. 1a. **Navigation structure varies by tenant — NEVER assume array shape.** Different tenants store nav in completely different structures. Getting this wrong causes `l.map is not a function` runtime error: ```typescript // Pattern A: Array (dayah-litworks) "navigation": [{ "label": "Inicio", "href": "/" }] // Pattern B: Object with navItems (nexa-paraguay) "navigation": { "businessName": "...", "navItems": [{ "label": "Home", "href": "/s/en/nexa-paraguay" }] } ``` **Fix:** Check with `typeof content.navigation`. For object with `navItems`, use `c.navigation?.navItems || []`. For array, use `c.navigation || []`. 1b. **Nav hrefs may contain PAB paths (`/s/en/nexa-paraguay/programas`).** After fixing the structure (step 1a), the href values themselves are wrong — they reference old PAB routes. Batch-replace in the header component: ```typescript const href = (n.href || n.path || "").replace("/s/en/nexa-paraguay", "") || "/" ``` Without this, clicking a nav link goes to `/sobre` (correct) but `prefetchNextRouter` on the `` prefetches the munged path (e.g. `/object Object`) causing `[object Object] 404` errors in console. The same `.replace()` must be applied in BOTH desktop and mobile nav loops. 1c. **PAB nav href prefixed with `/` + `.replace()` creates `//s/en/...` paths.** If your code does `"/" + href` BEFORE the replace, the link becomes `"//s/en/nexa-paraguay/programas"` which Next.js renders as `[object Object]`. The order must be: extract href → strip PAB prefix → prepend `/` if empty. 1d. **`@/` import alias may fail in freshly scaffolded apps.** The `@/*` path alias in tsconfig.json may not resolve correctly if `tsconfig.json` has `baseUrl` misconfigured or the file was copied from another project without regeneration. When `import content from "@/content/en.json"` fails with `Module not found`, fallback to relative imports: `import content from "../content/en.json"`. Apply this to ALL imports in ALL page and component files — header, footer, whatsapp-float, every page. The build error is a clean `Module not found: Can't resolve '@/content/en.json'` with file path. 2. **Next.js 15 `params` must be a Promise** ```tsx // ❌ BROKEN — Next.js < 15 export default function Page({ params }: { params: { slug: string } }) { } // ✅ CORRECT — Next.js 15+ export default async function Page(props: { params: Promise<{ slug: string }> }) { const params = await props.params } ``` Error: `Type '{ params: { slug: string; }; }' does not satisfy the constraint 'PageProps'. Types of property 'params' are incompatible.` - **Next.js 15 CSS import module resolution bug:** `Cannot find module or type declarations for side-effect import of './globals.css'` — This is a version-specific bug in Next.js 15.3.x with certain TypeScript configurations. Fix: **upgrade Next.js** (`npm install next@latest`) which installs 15.5+ and resolves the issue. The old fix (removing `import "./globals.css"` from layout.tsx) works but leaves Tailwind styles unloaded — the real fix is upgrading. Symptom: build succeeds but no CSS vars are applied in the browser. Also check for other Next-version-related issues like `next` binary not found in `npx next build` after copying node_modules from another project — do a clean `rm -rf node_modules && npm install`. 2. **Favicon must be in `app/` or referenced in metadata** Browser requests `/favicon.ico` by default. Put a file at `app/favicon.ico` or set `icons: { icon: "/favicon.svg" }` in `layout.tsx` metadata export. SVG files in `public/` won't be served as `.ico` unless explicitly referenced. 3. **Deploying a standalone app** ```bash cd /tmp/{client}/ npm run build docker build -t {client}:prod . docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml {stack-name} ``` Then add A record in Cloudflare: `{client}` → `72.61.44.159` (proxied). For dark sites with a LIGHT primary color (dayah: `--primary: #94abd6`), this overlay is translucent light-blue — **not enough contrast** for white-ish text. The fix was two-part: 1. **Component change** (`hero-section.tsx`): Replace the baked overlay with a configurable `overlayColor` prop, defaulting to `rgba(10,10,20,0.85)`: ```ts const backgroundStyle = backgroundImage && !useResponsivePicture ? { backgroundImage: `linear-gradient(${overlayColor || 'rgba(10,10,20,0.85)'}, ${overlayColor || 'rgba(10,10,20,0.85)'}), url(${backgroundImage})` } : undefined ``` 2. **Content change** (`es.json` / `en.json`): Add `"overlayColor": "rgba(10,10,20,0.88)"` to the hero content block. Without `overlayColor` in content, the component defaults to a dark overlay. ## Extraction

The nudo site existed in PAB at `sites/nudo/` with:
- `site.json` — config with social links, location, features
- `content/es.json` — full content with `/s/es/nudo/*` PAB paths
- `content/band-bio.md` — short + long bio
- `pages/*.json` — 7 page configs
- `images/` — 55+ images (brand, backgrounds, gallery, social)
- `ASSETS_INVENTORY.md`, `QUESTIONNAIRE.md`

Built as a **standalone app from scratch** (not copied from template) because:
- No existing template produced band-specific pages
- Content/es.json structure was PAB-specific with `/s/es/nudo` paths
- Needed custom pages: lyrics, shows calendar, Spotify embed, video gallery

## Key Differences From E-Commerce/Service Sites

| Aspect | E-Commerce (dayah/elviajero) | Band (nudo) |
|--------|------------------------------|-------------|
| Main CTA | WhatsApp ordering | Spotify listen + Instagram follow |
| Content | Products, services, pricing | Discography, lyrics, shows, videos |
| Media | Product photos | Spotify embed, YouTube iframe, gallery |
| Social | WhatsApp primary | Instagram + Facebook + Spotify + YouTube + Apple Music |
| Newsletter | "Subscribe for discounts" | "Subscribe for show dates" |

## DNS — IDN (Internationalized Domain Name)

The user added `nüdo.paragu-ai.com` as the DNS record (with umlaut). Cloudflare handles IDN natively. Traefik's `Host()` rule supports `ü` directly:

```yaml
- "traefik.http.routers.nudo.rule=Host(`nudo.paragu-ai.com`) || Host(`nüdo.paragu-ai.com`)"
```

No punycode conversion needed. Cloudflare resolves `nüdo.paragu-ai.com` to CF edge IPs. The punycode equivalent `xn--ndo-bma.paragu-ai.com` also works.

## Pages Created

1. **Home** — hero with headline/subheadline/trust badges, bio, features grid, Spotify embed iframe, discography cards, team profiles, testimonials, CTA banner, newsletter signup
2. **Música** — Spotify embed, full discography with track listings
3. **Videos** — YouTube embed gallery (extracts video ID from URL)
4. **Letras** — Song lyrics display (expandable, verse-separated)
5. **Shows** — Event cards with date/venue/description via Calendar icon
6. **Galería** — Photo grid with category filters (Todas/En Vivo/Festivales/Banda)
7. **Contacto** — Contact cards for WhatsApp, Email, Instagram, Facebook, YouTube, Spotify

## Components

- **header.tsx** — Sticky nav, mobile hamburger, Spotify CTA button (Disc3 icon)
- **footer.tsx** — 3-column: brand info, nav links, social links
- **whatsapp-float.tsx** — Standard green WhatsApp button

## Deploy

```bash
# Clone and build
gh repo create Ai-Whisperers/nudo --private
git clone https://github.com/Ai-Whisperers/nudo.git /tmp/nudo-site
# ... create files ...
git add -A && git commit -m "feat: Nüdo band site"
git push origin main

# On VPS
cd /root/nudo/repo && git pull
docker build -t nudo:prod .
docker stack deploy -c /root/nudo/docker-compose.yml nudo
```

## Traefik Labels — Deprecated Format

All existing standalone clients use `traefik.docker.*` labels. Traefik v3.5.3 logs:

